source.title: 2021 AHA/ACC/ASE/CHEST/SAEM/SCCT/SCMR Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain

signals: Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain

extraction.summary: This guideline provides evidence-based recommendations for evaluating and diagnosing chest pain in adult patients, both acute and stable presentations. The primary diagnostic approach emphasizes early risk stratification, appropriate use of biomarkers (particularly high-sensitivity troponins), and structured decision pathways to identify life-threatening causes while avoiding unnecessary testing in low-risk patients.

population.age_criteria: Adult patients (specific age range not defined)
population.risk_criteria: Patients presenting with chest pain or anginal equivalents including pain, pressure, tightness, or discomfort in chest, shoulders, arms, neck, back, upper abdomen, or jaw, as well as shortness of breath and fatigue
population.disease_definition: Chest pain evaluation focused on identifying ischemic causes, acute coronary syndromes, and other life-threatening conditions
population.exclusions: STEMI patients (covered in separate guidelines), specific exclusions not detailed in this excerpt
population.severity_staging: Low-risk, intermediate-risk, and high-risk stratification based on clinical presentation and pretest probability

recommendations.recommendation: Use high-sensitivity cardiac troponins as preferred standard for establishing biomarker diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction
recommendations.notes: Allows for more accurate detection and exclusion of myocardial injury

recommendations.recommendation: Urgent diagnostic testing for suspected coronary artery disease is not needed for low-risk patients with acute or stable chest pain
recommendations.strength: Class recommendation not specified in excerpt
recommendations.notes: Routine testing not needed for low-risk patients

recommendations.recommendation: Use clinical decision pathways for chest pain in emergency department and outpatient settings routinely
recommendations.strength: Class recommendation not specified in excerpt
recommendations.notes: Structured approach recommended for consistent evaluation

recommendations.recommendation: Include clinically stable patients presenting with chest pain in shared decision-making about risk, radiation exposure, costs, and alternatives
recommendations.strength: Class recommendation not specified in excerpt
recommendations.notes: Patient-centered approach emphasized

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with acute chest pain should seek immediate medical care by calling 9-1-1
recommendations.strength: Class recommendation not specified in excerpt
recommendations.notes: Early identification or exclusion of life-threatening causes is priority

recommendations.recommendation: Initial assessment of chest pain to triage patients effectively based on likelihood that symptoms may be attributable to myocardial ischemia
recommendations.notes: Essential for diagnostic process

recommendations.recommendation: Chest pain should not be described as atypical, but rather as cardiac, possibly cardiac, or noncardiac
recommendations.notes: More specific to potential underlying diagnosis

recommendations.recommendation: Obtain focused history including characteristics and duration of symptoms, associated features, and cardiovascular risk factor assessment
recommendations.notes: Should capture nature, onset/duration, location/radiation, precipitating factors, relieving factors, and associated symptoms

recommendations.recommendation: Women who present with chest pain are at risk for underdiagnosis, and potential cardiac causes should always be considered
recommendations.notes: Traditional risk scores often underestimate risk in women

recommendations.recommendation: In women presenting with chest pain, obtain history emphasizing accompanying symptoms more common in women with ACS
recommendations.notes: Women more likely to report ≥3 associated symptoms including epigastric symptoms, palpitations, jaw/neck/arm pain

recommendations.recommendation: In patients >75 years with chest pain, consider ACS when accompanying symptoms such as shortness of breath, syncope, acute delirium are present, or when unexplained fall has occurred
recommendations.notes: Older patients may have atypical presentations

recommendations.recommendation: Cultural competency training recommended to achieve best outcomes in patients of diverse racial and ethnic backgrounds
recommendations.notes: Marked racial and ethnic disparities exist in chest pain evaluation

recommendations.recommendation: Address language barriers with formal translation services for diverse patients where English may not be primary language
recommendations.notes: Vital to obtain accurate and complete history

recommendations.recommendation: ECG should be performed within 10 minutes of arrival for all patients with acute chest pain
recommendations.notes: Regardless of setting - office or ED

recommendations.recommendation: Serial cardiac troponin I or T levels should be obtained in patients with acute chest pain
recommendations.notes: To identify abnormal values and rising/falling patterns

recommendations.recommendation: High-sensitivity troponin is preferred over conventional troponin
recommendations.notes: Enables more rapid detection/exclusion and increases diagnostic accuracy

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with clinical evidence of ACS should be transported urgently to ED by EMS
recommendations.notes: From office setting

recommendations.recommendation: CK-MB and myoglobin are not useful when troponin is available
recommendations.notes: For diagnosis of acute myocardial injury

recommendations.recommendation: Focused cardiovascular examination should be performed initially
recommendations.notes: To aid in diagnosis and identify complications

recommendations.recommendation: Chest radiography is useful to evaluate for other potential causes
recommendations.notes: Cardiac, pulmonary, and thoracic causes of symptoms

recommendations.recommendation: Serial ECGs should be performed if initial ECG is nondiagnostic
recommendations.notes: Especially when clinical suspicion is high or symptoms persist

recommendations.recommendation: Clinical decision pathways should categorize patients into low-, intermediate-, and high-risk strata to facilitate disposition and subsequent diagnostic evaluation
recommendations.notes: For patients presenting with acute chest pain and suspected ACS

recommendations.recommendation: Use recommended time intervals for repeat troponin measurements: 1-3 hours for high-sensitivity troponin and 3-6 hours for conventional troponin assays
recommendations.notes: After initial troponin sample collection (time zero)

recommendations.recommendation: Institutions should implement a CDP that includes a protocol for troponin sampling based on their particular assay
recommendations.notes: To standardize detection and differentiation of myocardial injury

recommendations.recommendation: Previous testing when available should be considered and incorporated into CDPs
recommendations.notes: For patients with acute chest pain and suspected ACS

recommendations.recommendation: Single hs-cTn concentration below limit of detection is reasonable to exclude myocardial injury
recommendations.notes: For patients with acute chest pain, normal ECG, symptoms suggestive of ACS that began at least 3 hours before ED arrival

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with acute chest pain and <1% 30-day risk of death or MACE should be designated as low risk
recommendations.notes: Can discharge home without admission or urgent cardiac testing

recommendations.recommendation: For low-risk patients, discharge home without admission or urgent cardiac testing is reasonable
recommendations.notes: No evidence that stress testing or cardiac imaging within 30 days improves outcomes

recommendations.recommendation: For intermediate-risk patients, TTE is recommended as rapid bedside test
recommendations.notes: To establish ventricular/valvular function, evaluate wall motion abnormalities, assess for pericardial effusion

recommendations.recommendation: CCTA is useful for exclusion of atherosclerotic plaque and obstructive CAD in intermediate-risk patients with no known CAD
recommendations.notes: After negative or inconclusive evaluation for ACS

recommendations.recommendation: Exercise ECG, stress echo, stress PET/SPECT MPI, or stress CMR is useful for diagnosis of myocardial ischemia
recommendations.notes: For intermediate-risk patients with no known CAD eligible for cardiac testing

recommendations.recommendation: For intermediate-risk patients with acute chest pain and known nonobstructive CAD, CCTA can be useful to determine progression of atherosclerotic plaque and obstructive CAD
recommendations.notes: Applies to patients with luminal stenosis 1-49% on prior testing

recommendations.recommendation: For intermediate-risk patients with acute chest pain and coronary artery stenosis of 40% to 90% in a proximal or middle segment on CCTA, FFR-CT is reasonable for diagnosis of vessel-specific ischemia
recommendations.notes: Guides decision-making regarding coronary revascularization

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with acute chest pain and suspected ACS who are designated as high risk, ICA is recommended
recommendations.notes: High risk includes new ischemic ECG changes, troponin-positive, LVEF <40%, moderate-severe ischemia on stress testing

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with prior CABG surgery presenting with acute chest pain who do not have ACS, performing stress imaging is effective to evaluate for myocardial ischemia or CCTA for graft stenosis or occlusion
recommendations.notes: CCTA has 99% sensitivity and specificity for complete graft occlusions

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with acute chest pain where there is clinical concern for aortic dissection, CTA of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis is recommended for diagnosis and treatment planning
recommendations.notes: CTA is both highly sensitive and specific

recommendations.recommendation: In stable patients with acute chest pain with high clinical suspicion for PE, CTA using a PE protocol is recommended
recommendations.notes: CTA using PE protocol is the diagnostic modality of choice

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with acute chest pain and myocardial injury who have nonobstructive coronary arteries on anatomic testing, CMR with gadolinium contrast is effective to distinguish myopericarditis from other causes
recommendations.notes: Most effective if performed early, within 2 weeks of presentation

recommendations.recommendation: In patients presenting with acute chest pain with suspected or known history of VHD, TTE is useful in determining the presence, severity, and cause of VHD
recommendations.notes: TTE is widely available and good first-line test

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with recurrent, similar presentations for acute chest pain with no evidence of a physiological cause on prior diagnostic evaluation, referral to a cognitive-behavioral therapist is reasonable
recommendations.notes: 32% reduction in chest pain frequency with cognitive-behavioral methods

recommendations.recommendation: Use contemporary pretest probability models to identify low-risk patients for whom testing can be deferred
recommendations.notes: Prefer models from past 10 years over historical scores that overestimate CAD prevalence

recommendations.recommendation: CAC testing as first-line test for low-risk patients to exclude calcified plaque
recommendations.notes: CAC score of zero identifies very low-risk cohort

recommendations.recommendation: CCTA is effective for intermediate-high risk patients for CAD diagnosis and risk stratification
recommendations.notes: Preferred in patients <65 years not on optimal preventive therapy

recommendations.recommendation: Stress imaging (echo, PET/SPECT MPI, CMR) for intermediate-high risk patients
recommendations.notes: Effective for ischemia diagnosis and MACE risk estimation

recommendations.recommendation: PET preferred over SPECT when nuclear MPI selected
recommendations.notes: Improves diagnostic accuracy and decreases nondiagnostic results

recommendations.recommendation: Exercise ECG reasonable for intermediate-high risk with interpretable ECG and ≥5 METs capacity
recommendations.notes: Lower diagnostic accuracy than stress imaging but useful for prognosis

recommendations.recommendation: FFR-CT useful for coronary stenosis 40-90% in proximal/middle segments on CCTA
recommendations.notes: Guides decision-making regarding coronary revascularization

recommendations.recommendation: Optimize GDMT in patients with known obstructive CAD before testing
recommendations.notes: Should intensify medical therapy first when opportunity exists

recommendations.recommendation: ICA recommended for obstructive CAD patients with stable chest pain despite GDMT and moderate-severe ischemia
recommendations.notes: For guiding therapeutic decision-making

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with obstructive CAD and stable chest pain despite optimal GDMT, stress PET/SPECT MPI, CMR, or echocardiography is recommended for diagnosis of myocardial ischemia
recommendations.notes: Primary recommendation for ischemia detection

recommendations.recommendation: PET is reasonable in preference to SPECT when available to improve diagnostic accuracy and decrease nondiagnostic test results
recommendations.notes: When both modalities available

recommendations.recommendation: Exercise treadmill testing can be useful to determine if symptoms are consistent with angina pectoris and assess functional capacity
recommendations.notes: For symptom assessment and functional evaluation

recommendations.recommendation: Addition of MBFR to stress PET MPI or stress CMR is useful to improve diagnosis accuracy and enhance risk stratification
recommendations.notes: Adds prognostic value

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with prior CABG and moderate-to-severe ischemia on stress testing, ICA is recommended
recommendations.notes: Guides therapeutic decision-making

recommendations.recommendation: For known nonobstructive CAD with stable chest pain, CCTA is reasonable for determining plaque burden and progression
recommendations.notes: Assesses disease progression

recommendations.recommendation: For suspected INOCA, invasive coronary function testing is reasonable to diagnose coronary microvascular dysfunction
recommendations.notes: When persistent symptoms with nonobstructive CAD

recommendations.recommendation: Select tests most likely to answer the particular clinical question to minimize downstream testing needs
recommendations.notes: General concept to avoid layered testing which leads to higher costs

recommendations.recommendation: Consider tiered testing strategy starting with CAC scan followed by CCTA only in patients with detectable CAC or high pretest risk
recommendations.notes: Nearly 40% of patients avoided CCTA, reducing diagnostic costs with no events in this subgroup

recommendations.recommendation: Exercise ECG followed by selective stress imaging may be cost-effective for intermediate-risk patients
recommendations.notes: Tiered approach resulted in improved diagnostic accuracy and favorable cost-effectiveness ratios

recommendations.recommendation: Stress echocardiography or stress MPI are cost-effective for intermediate pretest risk patients
recommendations.notes: Improved CAD detection led to fewer office and ED visits

recommendations.recommendation: Use 12-lead ECG as initial diagnostic test for all chest pain patients
recommendations.notes: Should be performed promptly in emergency department setting

recommendations.recommendation: Consider 15-lead or 18-lead ECG for suspected posterior or right ventricular infarction
recommendations.notes: Important for detecting posterior wall and right ventricular involvement

recommendations.recommendation: Use high-sensitivity troponin assays for biomarker testing
recommendations.notes: More sensitive than conventional troponin assays for early detection

recommendations.recommendation: Implement 0-hour/1-hour or 0-hour/3-hour algorithms for rapid rule-out
recommendations.notes: Allows for faster disposition decisions in emergency department

recommendations.recommendation: Consider sex differences in symptom presentation
recommendations.notes: Women may present with atypical symptoms more frequently than men

recommendations.recommendation: Use chest radiography selectively based on clinical decision rules
recommendations.notes: Not routinely indicated for all chest pain patients

recommendations.recommendation: Use 1-hour algorithm for rule-out and rule-in of acute MI using high-sensitivity troponin
recommendations.notes: Multiple validation studies support 1-hour protocols

recommendations.recommendation: Consider 2-hour accelerated diagnostic protocol (ADAPT) for chest pain assessment
recommendations.notes: ADAPT trial showed efficacy for early discharge

recommendations.recommendation: Implement HEART pathway for emergency department risk stratification
recommendations.notes: Identifies patients suitable for early discharge

recommendations.recommendation: Use coronary CT angiography for intermediate-risk patients
recommendations.notes: Based on ACCF/AHA/ASE appropriate use criteria

medications.indication: Left ventricular opacification when ≥2 contiguous segments or coronary territory is poorly visualized

medications.class: Contrast agent with contraindication for allergy to iodinated contrast

medications.indication: For CCTA, CTA, and other contrast-enhanced imaging studies
Obese patients to improve imaging quality
Preventive care for INOCA and CAD progression
Chest pain evaluation (but response does not predict cardiac etiology)

red_flags: Life-threatening causes of chest pain requiring immediate evaluation
Acute coronary syndrome presentations
Symptoms requiring immediate medical attention via 9-1-1
Sudden onset of ripping chest pain with radiation to upper or lower back suspicious of acute aortic syndrome
Ripping chest pain described as 'worst chest pain of my life', especially sudden onset in hypertensive patient or with known bicuspid aortic valve
Shortness of breath, syncope, acute delirium, or unexplained fall in patients >75 years of age
ST-elevation on ECG requiring immediate STEMI protocol
New ST depression or T-wave changes suggesting NSTE-ACS
Severe chest/back pain with pulse differential suggesting aortic dissection
Signs of esophageal rupture (emesis, subcutaneous emphysema, pneumothorax)
Hemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia with diaphoresis)
Clinical deterioration or persistent symptoms with nondiagnostic initial testing
ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)
Acute aortic syndromes
Pulmonary embolism
Fulminant myocarditis
Esophageal rupture
Tension pneumothorax
Sickle cell chest crisis
High-risk unstable angina
Complicated ACS or AMI (<2 days)
Severe systemic arterial hypertension (≥200/110 mmHg)
Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis
Hemodynamically significant ventricular tachycardia or complete atrioventricular block
Moderate-severe ischemia on current or prior (≤1 year) stress testing
Significant left main, proximal LAD stenosis, or multivessel CAD on prior anatomic testing
History of prior coronary revascularization with worsening symptoms
New ischemic changes on electrocardiography
Troponin-confirmed acute myocardial injury
New-onset left ventricular systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction <40%)
Newly diagnosed moderate-severe ischemia on stress testing
Hemodynamic instability
High clinical decision pathway risk scores
Acute unremitting chest pain during dialysis
Clinical concern for aortic dissection
High clinical suspicion for pulmonary embolism
Acute aortic dissection with severe chest or back pain described as ripping or tearing
High clinical suspicion for pulmonary embolism in unstable patients
Acute severe valvular regurgitation with ruptured papillary muscle
Acute chest syndrome in sickle cell disease patients
Dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, unexplained iron deficiency anemia, weight loss, recurrent vomiting
Left main stenosis ≥50%
Anatomically significant 3-vessel disease (≥70% stenosis)
Severe stenosis (≥70%) in all 3 main vessels
Marked ischemia (≥2.0 mm ST depression at reduced workloads)
High Duke or Lauer exercise scores
Pathological Q waves with heart failure symptoms
Complex ventricular arrhythmias
Patients with sickle cell disease experiencing chest pain (associated with significant morbidity and mortality)
Exercise-induced VT/VF
≥2 mm ST-segment depression at low workload or persisting into recovery
Exercise-induced ST-segment elevation
Severe stress-induced left ventricular systolic dysfunction
Stress-induced perfusion abnormalities involving ≥10% myocardium
Stress-induced left ventricular dilation
STEMI requiring urgent door-to-balloon time reduction
Symptom onset to presentation delays in ACS patients
ST-segment elevation on ECG indicating STEMI
Acute aortic dissection findings on imaging or clinical assessment
Spontaneous esophageal rupture as underlying cause of chest pain
Cardiac arrest during self-transport for chest pain
High-risk clinical features for acute cardiac dissection
Persistent ST-segment elevation requiring immediate catheterization
High-risk troponin elevation with clinical instability
Hemodynamic compromise in setting of chest pain
extraction.follow_up: Not specified in this excerpt - full guideline contains detailed follow-up recommendations
extraction.escalation_path: Intermediate-risk and intermediate to high pretest risk patients benefit most from cardiac imaging and testing. High-risk patients require urgent evaluation. Specific escalation criteria detailed in full guideline sections not included in this excerpt.
